data.title: U.S. Equities In 2024: Invincibility Syndrome
data.description:
By Michael Grant

The excess returns delivered to investors in 2024 have been driven by substantially positive macroeconomic and policy surprises. Inflation has subsided despite resilient growth and earnings, allowing the Fed to declare monetary victory, and upbeat investors are projecting these favorable conditions into the future. How should we interpret the latest upward break-out by equities, with the S&P 500 pushing toward the 6000 threshold?

In our view, the paradox of this rewarding year is its underlying warning of low future returns for 2025 and beyond. There are those who expect the S&P 500 to push higher into early 2025 based on the belief that the only electoral outcome capable of disrupting this inevitability would be extended political uncertainty. If November provides a clear resolution, 2024 could go down as the most rewarding year for US large-cap equities of this century.

And yet, this thought pales in comparison to the most significant feature of this investment year - the perception of the virtual invincibility of US equities, which has emerged within large parts of the investment community. This is the conundrum: an “Invincibility Syndrome” signals a crescendo. It has historically emerged when markets are in the process of establishing a major summit.

A fair number of investors will agree that it is more challenging to identify major market tops than bottoms1. Major equity troughs are invariably accompanied by visible fundamental and psychological distress; fear can quickly become acute, whereas investor optimism can become a chronic condition. Equity summits are typically extended and marked by rotation as narratives across different styles and industry segments are pushed to exhaustion.

What all major equity tops - those of more than tactical significance - have in common is what we term the invincibility syndrome. This is more than an intriguing slogan. It can be drawn from data whose characteristic components have historically included valuation, sentiment and positioning. Each can be measured by widely available indicators when approaching extended or unsustainable levels.

The metrics that signal elevated valuation are so familiar that they need little review. The standard Shiller cyclically adjusted P/E ratio for US equities has risen to over 35, the third highest on record and only surpassed when bond yields were unsustainably low. The median P/E of the S&P 500 is 28X, last hit during the dotcom peak of 1999. Price-to-sales measures are back to levels last seen at the equity peak of 2021.

The nuance is that the “excess” relates to an island within a sea of greater or lesser value - specifically, the US technology-led growth universe. The leadership of this equity segment emerged in the years following the GFC.

It has been so overwhelming since 2014 that commitments to this US dollar-denominated style has become synonymous with equity investment for most of the recent converts.

A phenomenon of this magnitude should not be discounted as an egregious case of speculative enthusiasm. It is the consequence of the disintegration of the globalization era and therefore has a genuine underpinning.

In today’s new world of fragmentation, strategic risk has returned with a vengeance. It affects the fragile periphery of the world economy more than its core and international investors have voted with their feet, converging on the US.

In this light, there is an inevitability behind the strategic premium for American growth in today’s illiberal landscape of fragmentation, conflict and big government that characterizes the post-Covid world. US growth assets have generally benefited from this emergence of a premium as the globalization parenthesis of the neo-liberal era has closed.

For this very reason, we suspect that the US dollar is condemned to experience a large degree of overvaluation for most of this decade, contrary to received wisdom.

As long as conflict and protectionism are reasonable expectations, we believe capital will flow naturally to a US system whose strategic attributes have become more and more valuable. There’s a perception that only America can be considered militarily secure within its borders, benefiting from independence with respect to energy and most technological inputs and without undue reliance on exports.

Amid this, the US remains the predominant source of investment capital, especially for the technological innovation, which is a precious comparative advantage and stimulated by a US-China arms race.

The comparative undervaluation of ex-US assets is the other side of the coin. The resilience displayed by the US economy contrasts with the accumulating structural weaknesses of Europe, Japan and China.

We should therefore be careful in concluding that the rise in absolute and relative valuation of US equity growth is unsustainable if it is a structural consequence of “deglobalization” (or its political translation, “Reindustrialization of America”). Any diagnosis of today’s landscape should attach at least as much significance to investor positioning and sentiment as to equity valuation.

Sentiment and Positioning

There are numerous barometers of investor sentiment, some with long and reliable pedigrees. There are direct surveys of sentiment including those of the NAAIM2, Investor Intelligence and newsletters such as the “Hulbert Survey.” The general message is that confidence in the outlook for stocks has reached levels that have rarely been higher.

To cite one example, the Conference Board’s monthly survey of the American consumer includes a question about the prospects for stock prices over the year ahead. As shown below, the three-month moving average of this series has risen to its highest level on record. Newsletter writers monitored by the “Hulbert Survey” corroborate this: they have become more bullish than at any time since 2000.

Conference Board Consumer Confidence Expectation Stock Price Increase

1987 to 2024: 3-Month Moving Average

Source: Macrobond

Investor sentiment speaks little about the scale of risk, but extreme readings become significant when they signal the same message as measures of valuation and positioning. What is striking today is how positioning measures corroborate the diagnosis of extended confidence and valuation for the leading categories of US equities. What remains to drive a market higher if everyone is already bullish?

The allocations of global and US investors convey a similar message—that cash allocations are near the lowest levels versus history. This mirrors the message of cash on household balance sheets as a share of total financial assets3. On the surface, there is little cash to propagate financial assets or to cushion declines in the event of a shock.

1950 to 2024: Household Equity as % of Assets

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis

1999 to 2024: Cash Allocations by Global Investors

Global cash held by non-bank investors as % of total holdings of equities/bonds/M2

Source: JP Morgan Research, “Flows & Liquidity, how low are cash allocations?” October 9, 2024, using Bloomberg Finance LP, JP Morgan.

There is a popular idea that the ~$6 trillion of funds invested in money market assets is available to push stocks higher. However, when measured relative to equity capitalization, there is nothing exceptional about the current scale of money market funds4. Cash as a percentage of assets in equity mutual funds has reached historically low levels.

All of this speaks to vulnerability, but without answering the paramount question: What is the mechanism of trend reversal in the language of macroeconomics? In the context of the “strategic premium” earned by US equities, what are the “catalysts” and the related question of "how"?

Catalysts Always Emerge

The kernel of our answer is straightforward: today’s investment world is the prisoner of the assumptions of quasi-price stability and monetary dominance. In our view, this is gone whatever the central bankers may claim. In its place is an illiberal world of conflict, government interventionism, protectionism, populism and fiscal dominance.

There is a tenacious belief that only a recession is capable of killing the current bull market. After all, the inflation surge of 2022 failed to do so. Yet, the counterpart of the invincibility syndrome is today’s economic wishful thinking - most likely around inflation and interest rates. The bear market of 2000–2003 highlights that recession can be a consequence rather than a cause of asset price deflation.

The investment consensus expects earnings growth for the S&P 500 through 2025 of 10%–15% based upon an assumption of sustained economic growth. In this scenario, there is no value in longer-dated US Treasuries and the neutral Fed funds rate should not be below 3%. In the context of quasi-permanent fiscal expansion, the steepening of the yield curve should prevent the US 10-year yield from descending below 3.5%.

Put simply, the decline of long-term risk-free yields appears complete, unless the soft-landing assumption is badly wrong. The landscape taking shape represents the final stages of the bull market and a prelude to a much more disturbed period ahead, perhaps for many years.

Threshold 6000?

Amid the policy drama of the past quarter, the fundamental debates for 2025 have changed little. For the past year, investors have been discounting a soft landing with disinflation, while expectations of a Fed easing cycle have proven correct so far.

Recession worries seem premature, though moderating US economic activity is the central case into 2025. This is not a terrible outlook, just one that lacks the upside oomph to justify the S&P 500 forward multiple of around 21 times consensus earnings.

In an absence of economic stress, there are no significant novelties to exploit across the current equity landscape. This is why Fed easing may not matter much. Valuation spreads are not at provocative levels, sitting about half a deviation above the mean and signaling investor comfort with the benign and accepted fundamental outlook. We believe the rush to buy China-exposed stocks in recent weeks is an indication of investor appetite for new narratives when everything else has run its course.

China’s leadership is panicky. Its latest move implies that things are getting worse. Recognizing the scale of the problem is a start, yet the debt restructurings required to resolve this are complex and politically charged. Price momentum can be powerful for a time, but much of this is wishful thinking if China is not changing course on the big issues anytime soon. It will not operate according to Western norms.

The benign interpretation is that President Xi is tactical and the Party is pragmatic and policy is now bending to reality - much as it did with the sudden U-turn from zero Covid. A multi-faceted U-turn on economic policy could mark a watershed rebalancing of its development strategy. But this will be arduous and take years to execute, even if the leadership has the clarity and will amid today’s hostile global setting.

Today’s “invincibility syndrome” implies that when financial markets change their mind, they can do so in a profound way. And the one major insight may be that risks are more skewed to the downside in 2025 and a more diversified approach is appropriate.

Calamos has a long history of navigating such complexity with its risk-adjusted product suite. Our long/short mandates are now emphasizing alpha over beta, with the lowest levels of net equity exposure since late 2019.

1This excludes the cases in which exogenous shocks produce precipitous decline.

2The NAAIM Exposure Index is a weekly survey conducted by the National Association of Active Investment Managers, an organization representing investment advisors who utilize active management strategies to manage their clients’ portfolios.

3According to US flow of funds data, the allocation to equity of US households as a percentage of their total financial assets has risen to a record high of 34.7%. Major previous high points were registered by this metric in 1968 (29.5%) and 2000 (30.9%).

4In previous bear markets the ratio of MM funds to equity capitalization has risen to 30–50% compared with approximately 15% at this time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: MakeMyTrip: Lofty Valuation Leaves Little Room For Errors
data.description:
Introduction

MakeMyTrip (NASDAQ:MMYT) is India's leading online travel company. Based on the FY2024 annual report, the company's revenues are derived from: (1) air ticketing, (2) hotels and packages, (3) bus ticketing, and (4) other services such as railway tickets, car hire.

As a company that derives most of its revenue from India, MMYT is a good proxy for investors who are looking to gain exposure to the Indian market. Although the company is poised to enjoy multiple tailwinds, there is no margin of safety for investors at its current share price. In this report, I will demonstrate why investors should wait for a correction before they start to accumulate shares of this company.

Strong Growth Offset By Deteriorating Margins

In July 2024, MMYT posted a strong set of 1Q25 results. Total gross bookings have increased 21.60% year-on-year to $2.38 billion. Total operating profit surged to $39.1 million from 30.1 million in 1Q24, representing a year-on-year growth of 29.90%.

MMYT's gross bookings and adjusted margins across all of the segments demonstrated substantial growth. Gross bookings for air ticketing surged by 17% while adjusted margins grew by 21.2%. In the Hotels and Packages segment, the company posted revenue growth and adjusted margin growth of 24.70% and 29.60%, respectively. In the Bus Ticketing segment, gross bookings and adjusted margins surged by 15.9% and 20.70%, respectively.

Although the company posted strong year-on-year growth, margins have slightly deteriorated. Gross profit deteriorated to 66.30% from 69.50% last year, representing a decay of close to 400 bps. Net income margin declined to 8.20% from 9.60% last year, deteriorating by 140 bps. These deteriorations are primarily attributable to higher marketing expenses and procurement costs of hotels segment. It is also important to highlight that the company has only recently achieved positive net profit in FY2024; it is likely that we will continue to see volatility in margins, given that the company is still in its growth phase.

Macroenvironment Will Continue to Support MMYT's Growth

Today, India remains to be one of the fastest-growing economies. In the second quarter of 2024, India's GDP has surged by 6.70% year-on-year. Moreover, India's growth has outperformed other major economies such as China and Indonesia. S&P Global Market Intelligence expects that India will overtake Germany and Japan to become the third-largest economy in the world by 2030. By 2030, MMYT believes that India will add 140 million middle-income and 50 million high income households. Moreover, the Indian government has been pushing multiple infrastructure projects such as highways to improve domestic connectivity.

Apart from macroeconomic tailwinds, the online travel market in India has yet to mature; the company believes that the total market size will surge from $12 billion in 2022 to $60 billion in 2030, representing a market expansion of close to 400%. Moreover, despite being known as the world's technological hub, internet penetration rate and e-commerce rate is only at 55% and 23%.

Multiple Growth Drivers to Sustain Revenue Expansion and Maintain Market Lead

Currently, MMYT is the leading online travel agency ("OTA") company in India. As the Indian OTA market is still in its nascent stages, MMYT is focusing on multiple growth drivers to ensure that the company not only maintains its lead but also sustains its growth.

For example, in the company's Hotel and Packages segment, MMYT continues to expand supply by directly contacting hotels in key international markets for Indian outbound travelers. In addition, MMYT will continue to expand its offerings in alternative accommodations, providing a wide range of options ranging from villas, apartments, and homestays. To provide a comprehensive experience, the company is offering ancillary services such as currency conversion, trip planning, and visa application services for international travelers.

Finally, MMYT has expanded internationally beyond just India. In an effort to attract the Indian diaspora, the company's services will be accessible across 150 countries, including major travel markets such as the United Kingdom, and Europe. Previously, the company operated only in India, United States, and the United Arab Emirates.

Although MMYT Outperforms Global Peers, The Company is Relatively Expensive

Based on a comparable company analysis, MMYT has outperformed the industry median on almost all financial metrics except for gross margin. Although the company has a lower gross margin as compared to its peers, the company has outperformed on net margin; in fact, MMYT had posted the second-highest net margin in its peer group.

More importantly, the company has a healthy balance sheet and liquidity profile. It has a debt to equity ratio of only 0.20 as compared to the industry's median of 1.04. Moreover, its current Ratio and cash Ratio has outperformed the industry median by more than 2x, indicating that the company has ample liquidity to cover their short-term liabilities.

That being said, MMYT is relatively expensive as compared to its industry peers. The company is currently trading at a P/E and EV/EBITDA of 52.39x and 86.94x, suggesting that much of the company's growth may have already been priced in.

Company's Valuation is Extremely Lofty, Leaving Little Room for Errors

As noted above, the MMYT is still in its growth phase; hence, it is likely that we will see volatility in not only the company's operating margins but also the cashflows. Nevertheless, in an attempt to understand what the market is factoring into MMYT's share price, I have built a DCF model based on the following assumptions: (1) gross margins to remain elevated at 72%, (2) SG&A expenses to slowly deteriorate from 53% in FY2024 to 28% by FY2030, and (3) terminal growth rate of 2%.

Based on these assumptions and using the current share price of $104.7, the DCF model suggests that the market is pricing in an annualized growth rate of 35% between FY2025 and FY2030.

It is important to reiterate that although the company has posted strong growth in its recent quarter and has multiple growth drivers to leverage on, its revenue growth has already normalized. In the past four quarters, the company's average revenue growth was 30%. There is a strong likelihood that the company will not be able to maintain its growth beyond 30%.

Given that the assumptions used in the model were extremely optimistic, and the current share price implies that the market is pricing in a 35% annualized growth rate for the next five years, there is little to no margin of safety for investors; MMYT has little room for errors. Any slight hiccups in the company's growth trajectory is likely to cause a massive dump in its shares.

Closing Remarks

Overall, MMYT shows the characteristics of a strong growth company that is further supported by enduring macroeconomic and fiscal-related tailwinds. Moreover, the company has proved to be aggressive and successful in capturing growth and ensuring that they will maintain as the leading OTA in India.

Unfortunately, the company's valuation is extremely lofty. As compared to MMYT's industry peers, the company is considered expensive across all types of valuation ratios from P/E, P/B, and even EV/EBITDA. In addition, my DCF analysis suggests that the market is pricing in extremely optimistic and generous assumptions, leaving no margin of safety for investors at this time. That being said, investors should continue to closely monitor the share price of MMYT; it may be worth picking it up if the share price corrects itself.

MMYT is scheduled to release its 2Q25 earnings on 29th October 2024. Investors should monitor the company's growth developments. This company will no longer be viable if the company faces challenges in capturing growth or is unable to sustain its growth.

data.title: Apogee Enterprises: Market Is Too Conservative On Glass Segment Margins, Resulting In Undervaluation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome ABI Invest as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Apogee Enterprises, Inc. (NASDAQ:APOG) has done a good job in terms of transforming from a mid-single-digit operating margins business a couple of years back to low double-digit operating margins currently by implementing its continuous improvement initiative. In particular, the company's Architectural Glass Segment margins have seen a massive improvement, increasing from 2.6% operating margin in Q1 FY22 to 23.4% operating margin in the last quarter, and is running much higher than management's long-term target of 10-15%. While the sell-side is modelling this margin to revert to the long-term target range, I believe cost reduction and premiumization initiatives have resulted in sustainable improvement in margins and there is a good likelihood of management's long-term margin targets getting revised upwards rather than margins reverting to the current target levels. This should help the company post better-than-expected earnings and drive the stock re-rating. The recently commenced bolt-on M&A strategy should also improve growth prospects and help the stock become a good compounding story moving forward, driving the stock upside.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Apogee manufactures architectural glasses for commercial buildings (office buildings, hotels and retail centers), institutional buildings (education facilities, health care facilities and government buildings), transportation facilities (airports and transit terminals), and multifamily residential buildings. It also sells architectural frames for glasses and has an architectural services business under which it provides services like installation of glasses and frames, etc. In addition, the company has a small LSO segment in which it provides value-added coated glass and acrylic used in the custom picture-framing market, museum market, and various technical glass applications.

The revenue story over the last couple of years is pretty generic on the face of it. After seeing good growth in FY22 and FY23 when the interest rates were low and non-residential construction activities somewhat healthy, the company's revenue growth turned negative in recent quarters as higher interest rates and tighter lending standards impacted the commercial segment of the non-resi construction market.

However, what is interesting is that the company has done a good job in terms of focusing on premium products in recent years and thanks to the premiumization focus it has seen a good price/mix increase. The company has also focused on increasing its revenues from institutional (education, healthcare) and transportation (airport) markets, which are doing reasonably well.

In the second quarter of 2024, the company continued to see softness in key end markets like commercial, office, and multifamily residential construction markets, resulting in lower volumes. The company also exited some low-margin product lines under its "Project Fortify" strategy, which also impacted its sales growth. These negative factors were partially offset by improved pricing and product mix in Architectural Glass and favorable project mix in Architectural Services. As a result, the company's net sales declined by 3.2% Y/Y to $342.4 million.

Segment-wise, the Architectural Framing Systems segment's sales decreased by 11% Y/Y due to lower-end market volumes and the impact of moving away from the lower-margin product lines under Project Fortify. In the Architectural Glass segment, sales declined by 4.2% Y/Y due to lower end-market demand, partially offset by stronger-than-expected pricing and mix. In the Large-Scale Optical (LSO) segment, sales were down 16.1% Y/Y as a result of lower volume in the retail channel, which more than offset the positive impact from a more favorable mix. On the other hand, in the Architectural Services segment, sales increased by 11.3% Y/Y attributed to higher volumes and favorable project mix. Architectural Services backlog last quarter was $792 mn (up 17.5% Y/Y.)

Moving forward, the near-term headwinds are expected to continue as the company has exposure to the completion of non-residential buildings like commercial offices and multifamily homes (architectural frames/glasses are installed near the time of completion) and the non-residential starts over the past year have been impacted by high-interest rates indicating a slowdown in completions ahead.

However, I am not too worried about it as the interest rate cycle has started reversing, and the company can see a swift recovery in its end-markets in the coming years. Further, if we look at management's revenue guidance closely, they have guided for a 4% to 7% decline in sales (ex-UW Solutions acquisition) for the full fiscal with a 2 percentage point impact from one less week this fiscal year. This year will have 52 weeks instead of 53 weeks last year, and this one less week impact Y/Y growth number in the last quarter of the fiscal year. So, on a like-by-like basis, sales should be down between 2% and 5% for the full year. In the first six months (where like-by-like sales and actual sales were the same) sales were down ~5.8% Y/Y. So, the second-half sales trends on a like-by-like basis should be slightly better than the first half as per management's revenue guidance.

The company's sales should also see fewer headwinds as the Project Fortify initiative gets completed. Under this initiative, the company is divesting less differentiated, low-margin project lines and has completed most of the restructuring over the last two quarters. While Y/Y impact will continue to show over the next couple of quarters, as Apogee eventually laps these divestitures next year, sales growth should improve. According to management, this initiative is expected to negatively impact sales by 100 bps this fiscal, with most of the divestitures already completed.

The company is also doing a good job in terms of increasing its geographic presence, especially West of the Rockies. This geographic expansion should help the company's organic growth and market share.

One exciting development on the revenue side is that the company made its first major acquisition - UW Solutions - recently, after a gap of seven years. Management has communicated its strategy around bolt-on M&As to drive growth and market outperformance, and this acquisition marks the start of it. The valuation was reasonable ~8.5x FY26 estimated EBITDA (management estimates), and the EBITDA margins of UW Solutions are ~20% which is higher than Apogee's and in line with the management's plan to actively manage the portfolio and shift towards higher margin business. The acquisition should close sometime in Q3 and contribute ~$30 mn in sales in FY25 and $100 mn in FY26. the company ended last quarter with a leverage of 0.1x and even after the completion of this acquisition, its leverage would be ~1.5x, giving ample leeway for future M&As.

Over the long term, management has guided for revenue growth to be 1.2x its end market growth. Over the last few years, the company has done a good job optimizing its processes and developing and implementing the Apogee Management System (AMS) to improve productivity. With the interest rate cycle recovering helping the end market and the company implementing its organic and inorganic plans, I believe we should see good growth from the next year onwards.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

The margin story for the company is impressive, and the company has achieved a significant improvement in margins through cost-cutting and focusing on high-margin premium categories. In Q2 2025, the company's margins benefited from higher pricing, a more favorable mix of projects in Architectural Services, low material costs, and lower insurance-related costs. These positive factors were somewhat offset by the unfavorable operating leverage on lower volume, increased compensation and benefits expense, and higher restructuring costs related to Project Fortify. The company posted a 110 bps Y/Y increase in total adjusted operating margin to 12.6%.

On a segment basis, the adjusted operating margin expanded by 90 bps Y/Y in the Framing segment, 390 bps Y/Y in the Glass segment, and 450 bps Y/Y in the Services segment. Conversely, in the LSO segment, adjusted operating margin contracted by 100 bps Y/Y due to unfavorable operating leverage on lower volumes, partially offset by the benefits from an improved mix and cost savings initiatives.

Moving forward, the company's margins for Architectural Framing Systems and Architectural Services should benefit from savings from Project Fortify. Management initially guided for $12 mn to $14 mn in run-rate savings from this project, which they have increased to $13 mn to $14 mn on the last earnings call, with ~60% expected to be delivered in FY25 and the rest in FY26. The majority of these savings will be reflected in Architectural Framings Systems and Architectural Services.

The company has also done a good job in developing and implementing its continuous improvement initiative - Apogee Management Systems - over the last few years, and I expect continued focus on productivity improvement moving forward should also help margins. The margins in the company's Architectural Services are still lower than management's long-term target range of 7% to 9%, and I expect the company to reach this range in the coming quarters.

The company's Large-Scale Optical business has seen some decline in margins in recent quarters due to volume deleverage as well as investment in its capacity expansion project. The new capacity is expected to come online during the second half of this fiscal year, and additional volumes from it should help margins. Further, the recently acquired UW Solutions business will be clubbed with the LSO segment and this segment should benefit from cost and cross-selling synergies.

The story so far is well understood. However, the good execution and future cost improvement and growth prospects are not completely getting reflected in the stock price. The main reason behind it is concern around Architectural Glass segment margins, with sell-side modeling significantly lower margins for the segment in coming years, resulting in consensus EPS showing a decline next year. This is where we differ!

Management has done a good job in terms of taking cost out of the business and focusing on premium high-margin products, which has significantly improved the Architectural Glass segment's margins in recent years. The last quarter's margin of 23.4% was much higher than the long-term target of 10-15% that management shared. With near-term demand headwinds expected to continue in the back half, analysts are expecting it to reverse to management's target range. Management has also guided for margins to ease in the back half to the upper end of its target range.

But management has also said that they have "limited visibility" and they "love being wrong in the right way". Answering a question on the margin guidance on the last earnings call, the company's CFO Matthew Osberg commented:

...I mean, look, I love being wrong in the right way, and I've been wrong a few times on glass, but thankfully in the right way. As I tried to call out really just to give people some insights into that business, it's such a high variable contribution margin that as you look at what's in our pipeline and how that work will flow, it can really have a significant impact. And the good news for us is we've seen an extended hold-up of price and mix that's really buoyed those margin rates over the past couple of quarters. So as we look into the pipeline that we see now, which is limited, we've got some insights, but we can't still see the rest of the year."

I understand management is being conservative in terms of their guidance given the limited visibility. However, I find it problematic that the sell-side is just mimicking what management is saying without analyzing the situation for a more reasonable outcome. What makes the situation worse is that the sell-side is extrapolating trends in the back half of this year to future years as well. In addition to the back half of this year, sell-side analysts are also projecting margins to decline to management's long-term target range of 10% to 15% next year. This is resulting in the next year's estimates being too low (EPS showing Y/Y decline).

I believe this situation warrants a deeper look. Management has done a good job in terms of taking costs out and focusing on high-margin premium offerings to improve margins. Why would they suddenly give away so much margin, especially when the recovery is in sight with the interest rate cycle reversal already started? It's not that revenue trends are getting meaningfully worse. In fact, as discussed in the revenue section, like-for-like revenue trends are actually expected to get slightly better (or less bad) in the second half as per management guidance. I do sometimes understand it makes sense to give away margins to gain additional revenues in order to maximize EBIT dollars. But a reversal to management's long-term target range, even at the upper end, would mean an 840 bps decrease in margins from Q2 levels. This doesn't make business sense. If we do the math, to get similar EBIT dollars, revenues at 15% EBIT margins need to be 56% higher than revenues at 23.4% EBIT margins.

I understand the high variable contribution margin in the glass segment impacting on the downside. However, with recovery in the end-market demand in sight with interest rate cycle reversal, wouldn't the Glass segment start seeing meaningful margin benefits in FY26 as revenues recover? Most of the company's initiatives like focusing on high-margin premium products, and cost takeouts are permanent in nature and there is no logical reason to believe that these benefits would not continue in the future years. The sell-side projections of the Glass margins reverting to the 10-15% target range for FY26 make little sense, especially with volume recovery in sight. Even if we try to justify low glass segment margin assumptions for the back half (which I don't agree with), there is no way margins can stay at those levels in the future years as revenues recover. Management has not said anything about FY26 margins, and the sell-side is assuming the margins to remain low based on management's long-term targets. However, management has revised their long-term margin targets upwards multiple times, and the sell-side is likely in for a surprise again.

One argument behind lower margins in the back half is the demand slowdown and volume decline. However, volume has been declining for the last few quarters, but margins have seen a nice growth. The margins were up 270 bps Y/Y for the glass segment with a double-digit revenue decline in Q1 and up 450 bps Y/Y in Q2 with a mid-single-digit revenue decline. With continuous improvement initiatives continuing to help in productivity improvement, why would the margin decline significantly and stay lower in future years?

Overall, I see a good likelihood of management revising their long-term Architectural Glass margin target upwards. Management has a good track record of delivering better than promised and has already revised upward their long-term margin goals a few times. Last year, in Q4, management revised upwards the long-term margin targets for Architectural Framing Segment from 9-12% to 10-15%. Before that, in Q1FY24, management revised the long-term margin target range for Architectural Glass from 7-10% to 10-15%. I believe further positive revisions are likely.

The company has a good track record of beating sell-side estimates and given where the current projections are I expect this trend to continue.

Even if we assume, the company gives a couple of hundred basis points of margins to gain share and maximize EBIT dollar as demand remains slow over the next couple of quarters, in my opinion, there are no reasonable scenarios of glass segment margin reverting to management target levels of 10-15%. Given the recent progress that management has made in margin improvement of the glass segment, the medium to long-term operating margin target for the Glass segment should at least be 20% plus with a reasonable base-case scenario of it being in line with the last quarter levels (~23%), especially as the revenue recovery takes hold.

Overall, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. I believe the sell-side here is not definitely up to the mark in their analysis, and assuming the margin to revert to the long-term target range provided by management, rather than management increasing the margin target range, is resulting in too low EPS estimates.

Estimates and Valuation

If we look at the current EPS estimates, the sell-side is modeling $5.08 in EPS for FY25 and $5.01 in EPS for FY26. The company has already posted an adjusted EPS of $1.41 and $1.44 in Q1FY25 and Q2FY25, respectively. So, analysts are expecting a sharp slowdown in EPS growth in the back half.

The sell-side estimate also implies EPS reduction in FY26 despite UW Solutions business being accretive to EPS next year, continued incremental savings in Project Fortify (60% of $13 mn to $14 mn run-rate cost-saving target to be realized this year, and remaining incremental savings next year), improving margins for both Architectural Framing and Architectural Services segment (which are the two largest segment in terms of revenues), and improved revenue outlook with the interest rate cycle reversal helping next year revenues.

As explained before, the main reason for FY26 consensus estimates being lower is that the sell side is expecting the glass segment margin to revert to the management target range. This is unlikely, and I see a very slim chance of glass segment EBIT margins being lower than 20% in FY26 (my bear case scenario). My base case scenario for the glass segment EBIT margin is ~23% (similar to the last quarter's levels.)

Below I present a simplified model for FY26 P&L with consensus estimates (reverse-engineered, so may not be perfectly accurate but should be in the ballpark) as well as my bear and base case scenarios. I expect the UW Solution to be only marginally accretive. So, I have removed its Revenue/EBITDA contribution and its corresponding debt/interest expense for simplicity. Excluding a $100 mn revenue contribution from UW, the current consensus revenue estimates are building in $1.35 bn in revenue for FY26 and I have taken this same number in all three cases as the main difference between my thesis and consensus opinion is not about revenues. I have assumed EBIT margin improvement for both Architectural Framing and Services segments, with "Project Fortify" and continuous improvement helping. For LSO, I have kept margins at 20% with the new plant opening expected to help. Interest, and other income numbers are annualized from the last quarter, while the tax rate is assumed to be 25%.

The only difference is the Glass segment EBIT margins. It seems consensus is building it around 14% for FY26, while I have shared my calculations with 20% and 23% EBIT margins.

We can see my EPS estimates are higher than consensus by $0.73 and $1.10 in bear and base case scenarios.

The stock has traded at 13.4x average forward P/E over the last 5 years. However, it has come a long way in the last couple of years from a mid-single-digit margin business to a low double-digit margin business which is seeing further ongoing improvements. With margin slated to improve further and the company's recent focus on bolt-on M&A strategy to drive growth, I see further potential for re-rating.

Below are my valuation scenarios,

Bear case: The stock continues to trade at the current P/E of 15.40x (around the current levels) with the architectural segment posting ~20% EBIT margins in FY26 and the company posting $5.74 in FY26 EPS (see table above). This gives a target price of $88.4.

Base case: The stock re-rating continues further as the market realizes the sustainability of margin improvement across the segments and the company's bolt-on M&A strategy accelerates growth. High-quality industrial compounders with similar bolt-on acquisition strategies and continuous improvement initiatives have given investors phenomenal returns in the past - Danaher (DHR) being the best example. Usually, as these companies continue to execute well and the market realizes their potential for long-term compounding growth, they trade in P/E in the mid-to-high 20s. Apogee's execution so far has been impressive, and the stock has seen some re-rating. However, worries about margin compression in the Architectural Glass segment are still resulting in investors' hesitancy in giving the company full credit for this impressive execution. In my base case scenario, I am not expecting the company's P/E to re-rate to the mid/high 20s right away, but as APOG continues to post impressive margins and move forward with bolt-on M&As, I believe it is reasonable to assume the P/E multiple can re-rate to the low 20s. I see a 23% EBIT margin for Apogee likely in my base case, with the interest rate cycle helping the end market recovery. We have $6.11 in EPS in this case (see table above). Applying 21x P/E, we get a price target of $128.31.

Bull case: As APOG continues to execute well, the stock should re-rate in line with high-quality industrial compounders to mid-20s P/E. Applying 25x P/E to $6.11 in EPS (same as the base case scenario), we get a target price of $152.75.

Catalysts

I see a good likelihood of upward revision in earnings estimates and earnings beat. Further, as investors realise the sustainability of the glass segment margins and management revises their long-term targets upwards, the stock should see a re-rating in its valuation multiple. In addition, as the company announces further bolt-on acquisitions, investors' perception of the company's longer-term growth prospects should improve. I expect these factors to serve as a catalyst for the stock moving forward.

Risks

Bolt-on M&As are relatively riskier than organic growth as there are integration risks, risks related to overpaying, and from leverage taken for acquisition. The company's recent acquisition was at a reasonable valuation, and the cost synergy targets of ~$5 mn is not that high. So, I am not that worried about this acquisition. However, in the future, management needs to maintain this discipline with M&As, or else there can be a risk.

Conclusion

Apogee Enterprises' margin improvement in the Architectural Glass segment is sustainable and, even in the case of slower demand, only a modest decline in margins should be there. In the medium to long run, it is much more likely that the management revises their long-term margin targets for the segment upward, rather than the segment's margins reverting to the current long-term target levels. Management has a track record of executing better than targets and has already increased long-term targets for its Architectural Framing segment and Glass segment last year. I see further upward revision likely.

The current sell-side estimates are modelling a decline in EPS next year as they model significantly lower glass segment margins. However, I believe next year's EPS number can be meaningfully better than the current estimates. Further, in addition to continuous improvement and cost-cutting, the company has recently started implementing a bolt-on acquisition strategy which should aid the growth and help Apogee become a good compounding story moving forward. The valuation multiple is still reasonable because the market remains worried about the EPS decline next year. Over the next few quarters, as the fears about glass segment margins fade and the company delivers better-than-expected results, the stock can see a significant upside.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Value Idea investment competition, which ran through October 14.

data.title: Vale Production Report Q3 2024: Good Volumes, Bad Prices
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying Vale (NYSE:VALE) shares after the release of its 3Q24 production and sales report on October 15. The company is expected to release its 3Q24 results on October 25.

It is worth noting that this type of report provides many clues about how the company will release its quarterly results. The report came with no surprises, with a sequential increase in volumes offset by lower prices.

However, with the company trading at 3.5x EBITDA against a historical 5x, and with a more positive momentum due to a flow of positive news coming from China, the risk/return ratio of the investment still seems attractive.

Introduction

This report is a continuation of my initial article covering Vale, published on April 17, 2024, with the same recommendation. It is worth noting that Vale is the largest mining company in Brazil and one of the largest in the world.

The company stands out for the quality of its iron ore and for having China as the destination for more than 50% of its sales. Below, I will describe the insights I gained from analyzing Vale's 3Q24 production and sales report. Enjoy your reading!

Iron Ore Production and Sales

Vale reported an increase in iron ore fines production of 5.5% y/y and 12.9% q/q to 90.9 Mt, a record production since 2018. But as we can see below, there was a gap between volumes and sales, as sales reached 69 Mt.

On the other hand, realized prices fell 7.7% q/q and 13.8% y/y to $90.6/t due to lower iron ore prices in the quarter. The quality premium was negative (-$1.9/t), but improved from the previous quarter.

In my view, it is interesting to see how resilient demand is, given that Vale is managing to increase production even in a hostile environment. Additionally, I believe that the positive flow of news coming from China (responsible for more than 50% of Vale's sales) may provide some support for iron ore prices.

Vale's net revenue is expected to be $9.7 billion in 3Q24. As for prospects, if Chinese stimulus actually increases the price of iron ore, Vale could sell its stocks well in 4Q24, which would be positive and corroborate the buy recommendation.

Copper and Nickel

Copper and nickel production are still underrepresented. In copper, production of 85.9 kt grew +5% y/y, with the annualized figure representing 97% of the mid-range of the 320-355 kt guidance for 2024.

In nickel, production of 47.1 kt grew 12% y/y, with the annualized figures representing around 95% of the average guidance range of 153-168 kt for 2024. Neither of these figures shows any negative signs for the result.

Outlook For Quarterly Results

I believe that cash cost per ton (c1/t) should remain between $21 and $22, positively impacted by higher production with dilution of fixed costs, and negatively offset by the higher freight prices seen recently.

I also believe EBITDA should be close to $3.7 billion, down around 10% y/y. Although cash generation will be boosted by volume, the drop in iron ore prices should more than compensate.

Finally, the recent possibility of an agreement to compensate for natural damages caused in Mariana/MG may contribute to a significant drop in net income for the quarter. However, I continue to have a constructive view when looking at the company's valuation.

Valuation Is Cheap

Vale continues with an extremely attractive valuation, as we can see below using Seeking Alpha tools.

Since the company operates in the mining industry, I believe it is appropriate to analyze the valuation using the EV/EBITDA multiple. In this case, Vale trades at only 3.5x EBITDA, which corroborates my investment recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

The first threat to my bullish thesis on Vale is that it is not a consensus. Seeking Alpha's quant tools, for example, indicate a recommendation to hold the company's shares.

Additionally, one of the reasons for the increase in volume was seasonality. If Chinese economic stimulus measures are not effective, there could be a combination of weak prices and volumes, which will penalize results.

The investment thesis for the company is complex, given Vale's exposure to the Chinese economy. In my view, investors should be cautious when conducting their investment analysis.

The Bottom Line

Vale released its 3Q24 production and sales report without any major surprises. The company showed a nice increase in production and has inventory to strategically sell as soon as prices recover.

The recent positive news flow from China, combined with the company's extremely attractive valuation, offset the weak prices and the lower demand for the quality iron ore that Vale offers.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying Vale shares. In my view, a company like Vale trading at 3.5x EBITDA seems like an opportunity that doesn't come around every day.

data.title: Tracking The Current U.S. Business Cycle In 4 Charts
data.description:
The US economy continues to defy the recession forecasts that received much attention in the summer. The primary drivers of the economic resilience: strong growth in payrolls and consumer spending.

By contrast, industrial production, after a strong start once the pandemic recession ended, has faltered recently. Meanwhile, the revival of personal income has suffered one of its weakest runs during economic expansions since 1970.

Despite this mixed picture, the economic outlook remains upbeat, according to a new survey of economists published by The Wall Street Journal: “Forecasters are increasingly upbeat about the economy’s prospects.”

The average forecast for the upcoming GDP report for the third quarter: +2.4%. Although that’s moderately below Q2’s +3.0%, a 2%-plus rise in output (if correct) is a respectable gain that minimizes the odds that a recession is near.

The resilience of the economy can be seen by the unusually strong rebound in employment from the start of the current expansion, which began in May 2020, according to NBER. As the chart below indicates, the rise of US non-farm payrolls since May 2020 has dramatically exceeded hiring trends during every previous expansion since 1970.

A key explanation for the strong snap-back is the depth of the pandemic contraction, and the fact that it unfolded swiftly. In any case, the labor market’s revival has been unusually strong and is a crucial reason why the economy continues to grow. (Note: All the charts below reflect data for expansion periods using start and end dates selected by NBER.)

A similarly impressive story applies to consumer spending, based on personal consumption expenditures. The sharp recovery has been a crucial source of economic resilience. The strength of spending has started to slow, but relative to previous expansions, the consumer sector remains relatively robust.

Personal income, by contrast, has been unusually weak during the current expansion. At this stage of the business cycle, only one previous recovery path was weaker. That’s a warning flag that deserves monitoring because it suggests that the robust pace of consumer spending may be vulnerable.

Finally, the recovery in industrial output, after a strong start, has been flatlining over the past couple of years. The optimistic spin is that the US is increasingly a services-oriented economy, and so industrial activity matters less.

Perhaps, but economists will continue to debate if the waning of industrial activity marks a warning sign for the near-term outlook and US economic resilience generally.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: LKQ Corporation: Insiders Are Buying, But I'm Holding For Now
data.description:
LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) is about to release Q3 earnings results next week, on October 24.

Considering the increased interest from institutional investors during the last quarter, and the decent amount of insider buying activity at the end of September, most shareholders are anticipating the upcoming earnings results.

In this article, I considered reviewing the recent financial performance of the company, with an emphasis on the headwinds and challenges that the company is facing.

These challenges led to the 27% decline in share price since April this year. Additionally, valuation ratios have cooled off since then, showing a better overall picture of the company.

However, I maintain a Hold rating for the reasons discussed below.

For now, I would like to start with a company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

LKQ is a global distributor of aftermarket, recycled, refurbished, and remanufactured parts for collision and mechanical repair shops. They also provide aftermarket products to improve vehicle performance and aesthetics.

The company operates in 4 segments. Therefore, I considered including below a breakdown of total revenue per business segment from the latest 10-Q.

Even though the company is based in Tennessee, it operates globally, with a significant part of its revenue coming from Europe.

Ownership-wise, management doesn't have enough skin in the game. At least, in my view. The latest 14A shows less than 1% of beneficial ownership among all 18 directors and executive officers.

Headwinds And Challenges

Let's start with dessert.

Repairable claims in the North American segment during the second quarter were down by 7% YoY, continuing a trend that began in Q1 2024.

Among the factors that contributed to this decline, I am not buying the weather argument provided by Justin Jude, LKQ's CEO, during the earnings call.

In the first quarter, we faced revenue headwinds from a decrease in repairable claims in North America which was influenced by mild and dry winter weather conditions.

In my view, the main driver for this decline is a combination of factors, including rising insurance premiums (between 20% and 26% according to this article), higher repair costs, and declining used car values. The last one could be the most pressing factor, considering that the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index (MUVVI) declined in 2024 by 5.3%.

I strongly believe that the combination of these factors, along with macroeconomic headwinds, like the gradual upward pressure on unemployment, could have led some vehicle owners to forgo repairs altogether, choosing instead to scrap their vehicles or delay repairs.

Another argument mentioned during the conference call that discourages me is the statement from the CEO that ADAS technology helps reduce accident frequency, leading to a reduction in repairable claims of 1% per year.

... ADAS or accident avoidance systems, we would start to see that reduction in overall repairable claims. We think right now, it's around 1%, and BCG confirmed that.

This is the BCG report if you are curious. In my view, BCG underestimates the complexity of real world conditions. I have three arguments against their thesis:

  • Over reliance on these systems can lead to inattentive driving. Personally, I feel safer driving a 1972 Triumph Spitfire (no ABS, no TCS, no antiroll cage) than in the copilot seat of the latest Tesla driven by someone who overestimates the efficacy of the ADAS technology. In my view, there is nothing like the feeling of danger to keep you alert behind the wheel at all times. You don't believe me? Read the next point.
  • The report assumes high efficacy across varied environments, but ADAS performance can suffer in adverse weather or unclear road markings. See this video to better understand what I mean (a perfect example of relying on tech vs your driving skills).
  • Finally, the cost of repairs for ADAS equipped vehicles may offset the savings from fewer accidents, especially when sensors and cameras are damaged or need maintenance.

Moving on to other pressures, the specialty segment which includes LKQ’s RV and marine businesses, also faced recent challenges, with a 2.1% revenue decline.

The RV market, being discretionary, is particularly sensitive to consumer sentiment and interest rates. High interest rates and general economic uncertainty have dampened demand for RV products, leading to softer retail volumes. However, I believe in a rebound in the RV market in the next 12 months, considering the recent interest rate cuts.

Finally, unfavorable commodity prices had a direct impact on the self-service segment revenue. Steel, palladium, and PLATINUM prices declined in the second quarter of this year, leading to a 26.7% revenue decline in this segment.

However, since then, the prices for these commodities seem to have recovered, so I expect an improvement in this segment during the third quarter of this year.

In regards to recent legal disputes, I found this one, where LKQ challenged General Motors claiming their patents were overly broad and stifled competition in the aftermarket parts industry. In May 2024, the US Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit ruled in favor of LKQ.

Outlook

I'd like to start with price action.

As shown below, the company has underperformed its sector, its industry, and the S&P 500 over the last 2 years.

From a technical analysis perspective, since March this year, the share price has declined by 27%, breaking the ~43 support level, and maintaining a downward trend since July 2023.

So, does it make sense to buy now? In my view, the answer is no. I will provide my rationale below.

A quick look at the income statement shows an increase in revenue over the past 6 quarters. However, this is driven by acquisitions, especially in the last quarter, with the integration of Uni-Select, which helped boost revenue by 11.8% despite a decline in organic parts and services revenue of 2.1%.

However, the revenue growth was not accompanied by a growth in profitability, as shown below.

The double-digit decline in net income over the last two quarters is particularly concerning to me. It seems that their restructuring plans and integration costs related to recent acquisitions are weighing heavily on profitability. In Q2 2024, LKQ incurred $49 million in restructuring and transaction-related expenses, compared to just $8 million in Q2 2023.

Moving on to the balance sheet, the company has a high net debt of $5.44 billion, which has increased by 58% YoY in the second quarter. Additionally, the company has a negative tangible book value per share since September 2023. Furthermore, cash per share has been on a decline since that date.

It looks like in Q3 2023, the company borrowed funds as part of financing for its acquisition of Uni-Select, which was valued at $2.1 billion. By the end of 2023, the company had outstanding revolving credit facility borrowings of $914 million.

I considered including below a comprehensive breakdown of their debt structure.

Debt Instrument Principal Amount Interest Rate Maturity Date
Senior Unsecured Credit Agreement
- Term Loan Facility $500 million 6.82% January 2026
- Revolving Credit Facility $734 million 6.68% January 2028
Senior Unsecured Term Loan (CAD) CAD 700 million 6.44% July 2026
Unsecured Senior Notes
- U.S. Notes (2028) $800 million 5.75% June 2028
- U.S. Notes (2033) $600 million 6.25% June 2033
- Euro Notes (2028) €268 million ($276M) 4.13% April 2028
- Euro Notes (2031) €750 million ($803M) 4.125% March 2031
Other Debt
- Finance Lease Obligations $93 million 5.02% Various (2030)
- Other Notes Payable $17 million 3.44% Various (2030)
Total Debt $4.334 billion

The nearest maturity is in 2026, with a principal of $500 million. A quick look at the company's public bonds shows healthy prices for their bonds, above $90.

Considering that the company's Moody's rating is Baa3 and the Standard & Poor's rating is BBB-, I don't believe (today) there is a risk of default, despite bad liquidity ratios (especially the cash and quick ratios).

Liquidity Ratio Value (as of June 30, 2024)
Current Ratio 1.75
Quick Ratio (Acid-Test) 0.70
Cash Ratio 0.09

Let's move on to the cash flow statement.

As shown in the chart below, the decline in operating and free cash flows since Q3 2023 is concerning, especially the YoY decline in the last quarter.

It looks like higher interest expenses (reaching $130 million for the first six months of 2024, compared to $88 million in 2023), restructuring expenses ($49 million), and a concerning $225 million increase in accounts receivables for H1 2024 are weighing on profitability.

Despite this decline, I admit that the recent insider buying activity is encouraging. At the end of July, The CEO, CFO, and several other insiders bought over $600k worth of shares. And, to be honest, the share price has been holding ever since in the $39 level. In my view, management has a high conviction in a turnaround.

And they don't seem to be alone. A quick look at the 13F filers over the last quarter shows a 90% decline in the put/call ratio. In my view, the increased interest from institutional investors seems to support the possibility of a turnaround.

To be transparent, these two are the main reasons why my rating is Hold, and not Sell.

Valuation-wise, the football field below shows that most of the valuation ratios are trading at a fair level, meaning they are trading in the middle of the 5-year min-max range.

Additionally, if we compare some of these ratios with the competitors, it seems that the company is in the middle of the range. (I've sorted the list in descending order by EV/EBITDA).

Conclusion

To wrap up, I maintain a Hold rating due to several challenges that the company is facing. The drop in organic sales, rising costs from restructuring, and higher debt levels are putting pressure on profits. The company is also dealing with weaker cash flow and lower repairable claims in North America due to falling used car prices, along with challenges in the RV and self-service segments.

That said, the recent insider buying activity and increased interest from institutional investors over the last quarter could be a hint for a turnaround. If the company manages to improve profitability, especially if it decreases restructuring and integration costs, then I believe there is a compelling case for this stock. Additionally, from a valuation perspective, it doesn't seem that the company is overvalued.

However, I'm sticking with a Hold rating until I see some improvements in profitability in the next earnings releases.

data.title: Expedia: B2B Segment Upside
data.description:
Here at the Lab, we are back to comment on Expedia (NASDAQ:EXPE). In our coverage, we provided two updates on the company. In 2022, we were long with a comps analysis between Q2 2019 Vs. Q2 2022 numbers "Something Doesn't Add Up," and in 2023, we projected a "Margin Expansion Story." As a reminder, the company owns several brands, such as Hotel.com, Howire.com, VRBO, Travelocity, Trivago, and Orbitz, that allow clients to book flights, accommodations, cruises, and rental cars worldwide. As already reported, Expedia is the world's largest online travel agency. Related to our long idea, our buy rating was supported by 1) new ChatGPT features to help clients plan travel experiences and vacations, 2) a supportive loyalty program to develop, 3) new cost-saving initiatives with brand unification, and 4) a deleveraging story.

Why are we still supportive?

After a year, we are still bullish for the following reasons:

  1. (Supportive results and higher market share). Starting from the Q2 results, the company beat Wall Street estimates across the board with a plus 0.5% in sales and 5% in adjusted EBITDA. Expedia anticipates Fiscal Year 2024 Gross Bookings to grow by 4% yearly and top-line sales by 6%. This is at the low end of the company's expectations but represents an acceleration compared to peers. Industry growth has decelerated since 2023, but Expedia.com has gained additional market share in hotel shares. This is confirmed by Q2 solid performance with a plus 20% compared to last year. Our analysis also confirmed that alternative accommodation is gaining more share than traditional hotels. This is supportive not only for Expedia but also for Booking.com. These two OTA platforms offer different types of alternative accommodations on their website, offering downside protections;
  2. (Solid trends ahead). Expedia at the fastest rate" since the 2023 Q1;
  3. (New CEO). When Expedia's prior CEO, Peter Kern, announced that he would retire, we were concerned about who would take over. With Mrs. Gorin taking the reins, she brings a decade-plus of experience scaling the B2B business. Investors are also generally optimistic about her. With the ongoing challenges in Vrbo, the early outcomes have been impressive, and the segment is returning to growth;
  4. (B2B Upside). Additionally, Expedia's B2B segment has been a source of solid performance over the last five years. This segment reported sales and adjusted EBITDA growth at a ~30% CAGR over this time frame (Fig 1). In the Q2 results, B2B top-line sales reached $1.0 billion YTD, an increase of 22% compared to 2023. This is a new upside in our list, and Expedia's B2B has a leading market position with economic moats thanks to its supply scale, competitive rates, and rich content. These new disclosures from Expedia highlighted the fact that the demand significantly increased. This was also supported by the company's credit card loyalty program. TAM for the B2B is at $1.2 trillion (Fig 2), and the company has done a great job signing partnerships at a healthy pace. For this reason, we project double-digit growth for this business in the short-medium term horizon;
  5. (Vrbo stabilisation). Businesswise, from the Q&A analyst call, we understood that the management will incrementally invest in marketing, especially in Vrbo. There was a stop during the re-platforming, but as reported by the CEO, this has been a tactical mistake. Therefore, we might expect a recovery. Vrbo's business has lagged amidst platform migration in the last two years. We estimate that Vrbo accounts account for just under 15% of the company's lodging bookings. That said, the company reported a growth in Q2. Whether the recent trends are sustainable is still unclear, but we might anticipate some stability.

Source: Expedia B2B Overview - Fig 1

Fig 2

Earnings Changes and Valuation

One of Expedia's underappreciated bright spots is its B2B growth. Here at the Lab, we believe there is ample opportunity to deliver. With these new disclosures, applying a reasonable multiple to an impressively growing B2B business, we see a B2C segment as inexpensive. Additionally, Expedia is consolidating its loyalty programs into One Key, and this tech stack should improve sales and client retention. While the company is guiding flat margins in 2024, Expedia has multiple avenues to drive operating leverage moving forward. Following the H1 results, we forecast $13.5 billion in sales in 2024 with a 14.8% core operating profit margin. In our previous number, we modestly increased Q3 and Q4 bookings thanks to better-than-expected VRBO trends. With impressive results from the Brand Expedia and the B2B business, the company is set for a solid year in 2025. Global tourism is predicted to grow at 5.5% until 2032. Expedia has an impressive double-digit growth trajectory, and even applying a lower sales growth rate (8% in 2025), we anticipate supportive margin expansion thanks to marketing leverage, Vrbo recovery, and a balance sheet with almost no leverage. In our number, Expedia sales and EBITDA are projected at $14.6 and $3.1 billion, respectively. Our team sees a path for positive multiple revisions in the future. In our numbers, in 2025, Expedia trades with an EV/EBITDA of 4.5x with a P/E of 8.5x. This does not represent a company with supportive fundamentals, a positive trajectory, and a growing B2B segment. Valuing Expedia with an EV/EBITDA of 11.0x, aligned with the company's historical average, we derive an enterprise value of $34.1 billion, and considering its current debt level ($1.6 billion), we arrive at an equity value of $32.5 billion. This implies a 60% potential upside vs the current stock price. Therefore, our target price is set at $248. Beyond the financials, we liked that the CEO called out improvements in traffic growth and app booking mix.

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) a stronger $ dollar, 2) lower-than-anticipated consumer demand, 3) ineffective online advertising, 4) potential share loss compared to Booking.com and Airbnb, 5) economic slowdown, and 6) value destruction M&A. Additionally, in our numbers, we project normalized sales for Vrbo bookings.

Conclusion

Our team continues to believe that Expedia stocks are meaningfully undervalued. The company has a rock-solid balance sheet and is inexpensive. There are supportive trends ahead and an internal growth engine to valorised. Our buy rating is then confirmed.

data.title: MARA Holdings: Mining Kaspa Doesn't Fix The Fundamental Problem
data.description:
Back in May when I last wrote about Marathon Digital Holdings (NASDAQ:MARA) for Seeking Alpha, I called the stock a 'hold' and made note of the company's edge over other mining pools in average block fees. I also highlighted the trend in production and industry profitability, among other things. Since then, we have some new catalysts that I think lend credence to the notion that MARA shares will ultimately serve as little more than trading instruments rather than a quality investment over time.

In this update, we'll look at MARA's 'diversification' of the company's mining footprint to another cryptocurrency, re-assess how MARA Pool is progressing against the competition, and consider the company's new BTC acquisition strategy.

Pivoting To Kaspa Mining

In late-June, MARA announced it had begun mining Kaspa (KAS-USD) in addition to its Bitcoin (BTC-USD) mining operation to diversify away from just BTC. In the press release, MARA mentioned the Kaspa mining strategy actually started in September 2023. Marathon also noted Kaspa mining margins of up to 95% in certain cases, given the price of KAS and the low network difficulty rate for miners. Marathon guided for full energization of its 60 petahash Kaspa ASIC purchases by the end of Q3. The chart below is quite telling:

Kaspa currently has a global hash rate approaching 1.2 EH/s. But what I find interesting is the timing of the enormous spike in KAS mining in mid-July. The global hash rate for Kaspa mining was 0.35 EH/s the day Marathon announced its Kaspa mining strategy. Global exahash is now roughly three times that level, and yet, the price of KAS is actually down 25%:

So any real Kaspa profit edge that MARA had in June before announcing its Kaspa strategy is likely gone at this point. It's three times more difficult to generate a coin that is worth 25% less than it was the day of MARA's press release. If the company's goal was to tout a profitable new business line, it doesn't appear that it has lasted very long.

Bitcoin Mining Challenges

I think it's important to understand that the very notion that MARA would mention Kaspa mining is not a small admission from the biggest public miner in the industry that mining BTC is a bad business:

Though it's slightly ahead of the lows from August and September, the US dollar-denominated hash price for Bitcoin has been under $50 per PH/s for most of the last 5 months. Bitcoin mining profitability is still very much near the lowest levels in the history of the chain. I've covered this ad nauseam at this point, but the fixed supply of the native asset means miners need higher BTC prices in perpetuity or a larger share of transaction fees as a percentage of total rewards.

I think it should go without saying, Bitcoiners would want to see the latter option be the way miners are incentivized going forward. So far, we're simply not seeing the stickiness of transaction fees for longer than a few weeks at a time. Since the April halving, transaction fee percent of total miner rewards has averaged about 4%. This is inline with what the average has been since the beginning of 2022. However, more recently, that average has dipped back below 2%.

To company's credit, MARA has taken steps to get more than its 'fair share' of revenue from transactions through its Slipstream product. I covered this in my last MARA article from May, and I would recommend reading that for more details. But the main takeaway is MARA Pool can generate more from transaction fees if users decide to go to MARA directly for transaction validation. In May, MARA Pool was getting an 11% bump over expected block fees, which, I believe, was a direct result of Slipstream.

Rank Pool Blocks Avg Block Fees

4 F2Pool 2784 (10.54%) -2.18% 6 (0.22%)
5 MARA Pool 1086 (4.11%) +0.65% 0 (0.00%)
6 Binance Pool 792 (3%) -1.73% 6 (0.76%)
7 SpiderPool 777 (2.94%) -6.45% 27 (3.47%)
